title: The Australian Emigrant
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How dazzling the sunbeams awoke on the spray,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When Australia first rose in the distance away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As welcome to us on the deck of the bark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the dove to the vision of those in the ark!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What fairylike fancies appearן¿½d to the view
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As nearer and nearer the haven we drew!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What castles were built and rebuilt in the brain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To totter and crumble to nothing again!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We had roamן¿½d oן¿½er the ocean had traversן¿½d a path,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the tempest surrounded and shriekן¿½d in its wrath:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alike we had rollן¿½d in the hurricaneן¿½s breath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And slumberן¿½d on waters as silent as death:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We had watchן¿½d the Day breaking each morn on the main,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And had seen it sink down in the billows again;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For week after week, till disheartenן¿½d we thought
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An age would elapse ere we enterן¿½d the port.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How often while ploughing the ן¿½watery wasteן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Our thoughts from the Future have turnן¿½d to the Past;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How often our bosoms have heavן¿½d with regret;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For faces and scenes we could never forget:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For weן¿½d seen as the shadows oן¿½er-curtainן¿½d our minds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The cliffs of old England receding behind;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And had turned in our tears from the view of the shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The land of our childhood, to see it no more.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But when that red morning awoke from its sleep,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To show us this land like a cloud on the deep;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when the warm sunbeams imparted their glow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the heavens above and the ocean below;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The hearts had been aching then revellן¿½d with joy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a pleasure was tasted exempt from alloy;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The souls had been heavy grew happy and light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all was forgotten in present delight.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis true of the hopes that were verdant that day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is more than the half of them withered away:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis true that emotions of temperן¿½d regret,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still live for the country weן¿½ll never forget;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But yet we are happy, since learning to love
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The scenes that surround us the skies are above,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We find ourselves bound, as it were by a spell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the clime weן¿½ve adopted contented to dwell.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Kendall represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Kendall's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
